text.title: Rate Limiting Implementation and Response Headers
text.summary: FastAPI middleware pattern, tiered limits, per-endpoint configuration, standard response headers, and 429 response shape.

rule.statement: Every rate-limited response must include X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp), and Retry-After (seconds) on 429 responses.
rule.rationale: Standard headers allow clients to implement polite backoff without guessing the reset window. Retry-After is required by RFC 6585 on 429 responses. Without these headers, clients resort to exponential backoff with arbitrary delays.

example.code:
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests X-RateLimit-Limit: 100 X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 X-RateLimit-Reset: 1735689600 Retry-After: 60 Content-Type: application/json
{ "error": { "code": "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED", "message": "Too many requests", "retryAfter": 60, "limit": 100, "window": "1h" } }

section.title: FastAPI Middleware

rule.statement: Implement rate limiting as an HTTP middleware layer, not inside individual route handlers. Extract the rate limit key from authenticated user identity, not IP address.
rule.rationale: Middleware centralises the enforcement point and ensures no endpoint bypasses rate limiting. IP-based limiting fails behind NAT/proxies where many users share one IP.

section.reference: FastAPI rate-limit middleware with header injection and 429 response

rule.statement: Define plan-based tiers (free/plus/pro/enterprise) with separate limit + burst values. Overlay per-endpoint overrides for expensive operations that warrant tighter limits regardless of tier.
rule.rationale: A single global limit under-serves premium plans and over-serves free plans. Per-endpoint limits protect slow operations (search, export) from consuming the full quota allowance.

antipattern.description: Using the same rate limit for all endpoints regardless of computational cost.
antipattern.reason: A search endpoint that hits a database and an AI model should have a tighter limit than a GET /health check; treating them equally wastes budget on expensive operations and wastes capacity on cheap ones.

list.item: Use Redis for distributed rate limiting — in-memory counters are per-instance only
Log rate limit events at WARN level with user_id, endpoint, and limit
Whitelist internal service-to-service traffic by API key or IP range
Return rate limit headers on all responses, not just 429
